g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Weak Entity Types in Database Management Systems. Let us begin with understanding this fundamental concept.
A weak entity type lacks its own primary key and depends on a strong entity called the identifying owner for unique identification.
g.text: A Weak Entity does NOT have a primary key of its own.
It depends on an "Identifying Owner" (Strong Entity) for identification.
Primary Key = Owner's PK + Partial Key (Discriminator)

g.data-narration: Weak entities model real-world scenarios where objects cannot exist independently, like rooms in buildings or dependents of employees.
g.text: Why Do We Need Weak Entities?
Real-world objects often cannot exist independently:
• A Room cannot exist without a Building
• A Dependent cannot exist without an Employee

g.data-narration: The identifying owner is the strong entity on which the weak entity depends. A weak entity can have only one identifying owner at a time.
g.text: The Strong Entity on which the weak entity depends for identification.

g.data-narration: The identifying relationship connects weak entities to their owners. It always has total participation, meaning every weak entity must have an owner.
g.text: The relationship connecting a weak entity to its identifying owner.
• Represented by a DOUBLE DIAMOND in ER diagrams
• Always has TOTAL PARTICIPATION from weak entity side
• The owner must exist before the weak entity can be created

g.data-narration: The partial key or discriminator uniquely identifies weak entities within ONE owner. Combined with owner's PK, it forms the complete identifier.
g.text: An attribute that uniquely identifies weak entities WITHIN A SINGLE OWNER.
Example: Dep_Name is partial key for Dependent. Employee John can have
only one dependent named "Mary", but Employee Bob can also have "Mary".

g.data-narration: In ER diagrams, weak entities use double rectangles, identifying relationships use double diamonds, and partial keys use dashed underlines.
Strong entities have independent existence and own primary keys. Weak entities depend on owners and derive their identifiers from the owner's primary key.
g.text: No own PK | Depends on owner

g.data-narration: The primary key of a weak entity is formed by combining the identifying owner's primary key with the weak entity's partial key.
In the Employee-Dependent example, Employee is strong with Emp_ID as primary key. Dependent is weak, identified by Emp_ID plus Dep_Name.
In the Building-Room example, rooms cannot exist without buildings. Building_ID plus Room_Number forms the unique identifier for rooms.
In e-commerce, order items cannot exist without orders. The primary key combines Order_ID with Item_ID or line number.
Remember these key exam points: weak entities use double rectangles, total participation is mandatory, and the primary key formula is owner PK plus partial key.
g.text: 1. Double rectangle = Weak Entity | Double diamond = Identifying Relationship
2. Total participation: Every weak entity MUST have an owner
4. Relationship is typically 1:N (one owner, many weak entities)
5. When owner deleted, weak entities are cascade deleted

g.data-narration: To summarize, weak entities depend on owners, use double rectangles in ER diagrams, and are identified by combining owner PK with partial key.
